title: Biostatistics for Clinical Research in Urban Planning
description: Learn biostatistical methods through the lens of urban planning research. Adaptive branching routes you to appropriate depth based on diagnostic assessment.

nodes.title: Biostatistics Fundamentals - Diagnostic Quiz
nodes.content: Test your understanding of biostatistical concepts essential for clinical research design and interpretation.

questions.question: A city wants to evaluate if a new urban green space reduces resident stress. They randomly assign 1,000 of 10,000 residents to visit the space or not, measuring stress levels. What is the 1,000 called in research terms?
feedback: The entire group of residents would be the population, but the selected 1,000 are the sample.
Correct! The sample is the group of individuals selected from the larger population.
The parameter is a characteristic of the population; in this case, the mean stress level of all residents.
A variable is a characteristic being measured (stress level), not the group itself.
questions.explanation: In biostatistics, a sample is a subset drawn from a population for study.

questions.question: A transit authority tests a new bus route in 5 neighborhoods and compares transit usage to 5 similar neighborhoods without the route. The neighborhoods without the route are serving what role?
feedback: Confounding variables are factors that affect the outcome but aren't controlled.
Yes! The control group allows comparison to determine the effect of the intervention.
Stratified sampling is a sampling technique, not a comparison group role.
While control groups do help reduce bias, that's not their specific name.
questions.explanation: A control group receives no intervention and provides a baseline for comparison.

questions.question: An urban planner reports that a new park design increases foot traffic by 25% (p = 0.03). What does p = 0.03 mean?
options: There's a 3% chance the park caused the increase
There's a 97% probability the finding is real
There's a 3% probability the result occurred by chance alone
feedback: The p-value doesn't tell us the probability of causation.
A p-value doesn't give us probability that the finding is real.
Correct! A p-value represents the probability that results are due to random chance.
The p-value is not the effect size; it's a probability value.
questions.explanation: The p-value represents the probability of observing results as extreme as ours if the null hypothesis were true.

nodes.title: Advanced Biostatistical Design for Urban Research
nodes.content: When evaluating complex urban interventions, researchers must consider advanced designs like matched cohorts, instrumental variables, and regression modeling. For example, when assessing whether increasing street lighting reduces crime, propensity score matching can account for neighborhoods that already invested in safety. Multi-level modeling recognizes that residents (level 1) are nested within neighborhoods (level 2) and cities (level 3), affecting outcomes. Interrupted time series designs evaluate policy impacts by examining trends before and after implementation. These advanced methods yield more robust estimates and account for real-world complexity in urban systems.

nodes.title: Case Study: Evaluating a Transit Policy Using Propensity Score Matching
nodes.content: A city implemented a congestion pricing policy in the downtown core and wants to measure its impact on air quality and commuting patterns. Neighborhoods naturally self-selected into policy exposure; those with high pre-intervention pollution were more likely to be affected. Using propensity score matching, researchers created matched pairs of neighborhoods based on pre-policy characteristics (population density, baseline pollution, income). This mimics randomization by comparing 'similar' neighborhoods exposed vs. unexposed to the policy. They found a 12% reduction in PM2.5 (95% CI: 8-16%), p = 0.001, controlling for weather variations via multi-level regression. The matched design strengthens causal inference despite the observational setting.

nodes.title: Biostatistics Foundations for Urban Research
nodes.content: Biostatistics in urban planning studies the health, behavior, and characteristics of city populations. A population is everyone; a sample is the group you study. Variables are characteristics you measure—like resident satisfaction, air quality, or transit usage. A study compares groups: intervention and control. Randomization randomly assigns neighborhoods to receive or not receive a program, reducing bias. The null hypothesis assumes no effect; if data contradict it strongly enough, we conclude the intervention works. A confidence interval gives a range where the true effect likely sits. Understanding these fundamentals lets you design fair studies and interpret results skeptically.

nodes.title: Case Study: Assessing Neighborhood Satisfaction After Park Installation
nodes.content: A city installed parks in 8 neighborhoods and surveyed resident satisfaction in those neighborhoods and 8 matched control neighborhoods without new parks. They randomly selected 50 residents from each neighborhood (total n=800). Mean satisfaction increased 3.2 points on a 10-point scale in park neighborhoods (95% CI: 2.1-4.3) vs. 0.5 points in control neighborhoods. Using a t-test, p = 0.002—very unlikely by chance alone. They concluded parks significantly improve neighborhood satisfaction. This simple design included sampling, random selection, a control group, and statistical testing—key biostatistical principles.

nodes.title: Practice: Interpreting Urban Studies
nodes.content: Test your understanding of biostatistical concepts in urban research settings.

questions.question: A city surveys 200 of 5,000 residents about a new sidewalk program. The 5,000 is the ___ and the 200 is the ___.
feedback: You've reversed them.
Exactly! The total group (5,000 residents) is the population; the selected subset (200) is the sample.
Control and treatment refer to group assignments, not population/sample.
Parameter and statistic are different concepts related to measures of the population vs. sample.
questions.explanation: Always distinguish the full population from the studied sample.

questions.question: Results show: 'Bike lanes reduced commute time by 4 minutes (95% CI: 1-7 minutes), p = 0.008.' What is the 95% confidence interval?
options: The range where the true effect likely lies (1-7 minutes)
The 95% of outcomes that are statistically significant
The standard error of the measurement
feedback: A confidence interval is not a range of p-values.
Correct! A confidence interval is a range of plausible values for the true effect.
Confidence intervals don't represent percentages of outcomes; they estimate where the true parameter sits.
A confidence interval is not the same as standard error.
questions.explanation: A 95% CI means we're 95% confident the true effect lies within that range.

questions.question: Why is it important to include a control group in urban research?
options: To reduce the sample size needed
To determine causation by comparing intervention vs. no intervention outcomes
To save money on the study
To ensure everyone gets the intervention eventually
feedback: Control groups don't necessarily reduce sample size.
Yes! The control group provides a baseline to attribute changes to the intervention, not other factors.
Cost is secondary to validity.
Control groups don't promise eventual universal access.
questions.explanation: Comparison is essential for causal inference in observational studies.

nodes.title: Course Complete: Biostatistics for Urban Planning Research
nodes.content: Congratulations! You've mastered biostatistical concepts essential for designing and interpreting urban planning research. Whether you took the advanced or foundational path, you now understand sampling, study design, control groups, and statistical inference. Apply these principles to evaluate real-world interventions—from transit policies to green space initiatives—using rigorous, evidence-based methods.
